Your May cash flow tells an interesting story — you brought in $13,960 from three client projects (Figma Design Systems, Notion UX Research, and a Stripe payment) and spent $5,503, generating a healthy $8,457 net. That $8,000 surplus question is timely and very answerable given what I can see about your setup.
As a self-employed UX consultant with no employer benefits, uneven income, and a quarterly tax obligation, the order of operations here is a bit different from a salaried employee's playbook.
Priority 1: Confirm your Q2 tax reserve is covered
Your April IRS estimated tax payment was $4,200. Your next quarterly estimated payment (Q2 2026) is due June 16 — that's 11 days away. Before anything else, I'd suggest verifying that your Ally Tax Reserve Savings account (which you use specifically for this purpose) already has enough set aside for that payment.
A rough self-employment tax reserve for someone at your income level typically runs 25–30% of net profit. If May's $8,457 net hasn't been partially reserved yet, setting aside approximately $2,100–$2,500 of your $8,000 for tax purposes first is the safest move. This is especially important given your preference for tax-safe cash flow.
Priority 2: Emergency fund health check
Since you have no employer safety net — no PTO, no severance, no unemployment — your personal emergency fund needs to be more robust than average. The standard 3–6 months of expenses applies, but for variable-income freelancers, 6 months is the right target. Based on your May spending of ~$5,500, that means roughly $33,000 as a target floor.
Do you have that covered across your personal accounts? If not, the next chunk of this $8,000 belongs there, ideally in a high-yield savings account earning around 4% APY rather than sitting idle.
Priority 3: HSA top-up (high-value move for you specifically)
I noticed you made a $300 HSA contribution in May to your Fidelity HSA. The 2026 self-only HSA contribution limit is $4,400. The HSA is one of the most tax-efficient vehicles available to you as a self-employed person with a marketplace health plan — contributions are pre-tax, growth is tax-free, and qualified withdrawals are tax-free. If you're behind on your annual contribution, directing some of this $8,000 there is worth serious consideration.
Priority 4: Solo 401(k) or SEP IRA contributions
Once taxes and emergency reserves are handled, this is where the real long-term leverage is for a self-employed UX consultant. You're eligible for a Solo 401(k), which allows up to $72,000 in total contributions for 2026 (employee deferral up to $24,500 + employer profit-sharing contributions). Alternatively, a SEP IRA allows up to 25% of net self-employment income, capped at $72,000.
Since your gross income data isn't in your profile yet, I can't calculate the exact maximum right now — but even $2,000–$4,000 directed here would compound meaningfully over time and reduce your Schedule C taxable income.
Putting it together
A reasonable allocation of your $8,000, assuming taxes are already reserved:
$2,000–$2,500 → Ally Tax Reserve (Q2 estimated tax buffer, if not already covered)
$2,000–$3,000 → Personal emergency fund top-up (if below 6-month target)
$1,000–$2,000 → HSA contribution toward the $4,400 annual limit
Remaining → Solo 401(k) or SEP IRA contribution
If your emergency fund is already fully funded and your tax reserve is solid, you could skip straight to maxing the HSA and then directing the rest toward retirement contributions — that combination is particularly powerful for reducing your self-employment tax burden.
Do you know roughly where your emergency fund stands today, or how much you've contributed to your HSA so far this year? That would help me sharpen the exact allocation for you.
